title: Corporate Relocation and Housing Market Spillovers
abstract: Using a comprehensive database of corporate relocation events in the United States from 1994 to 2017, we investigate the impact of headquarters relocation on the local economy by examining its spillover effects on the housing market. We find that headquarters relocation into a district at zip code level leads to 10% higher housing price growth. We also document a temporal spillover effect whereby housing prices increase one year before the relocation and rise further until two years afterward and a significant spatial spillover effect of corporate relocation on nearby zip codes’ housing markets up to 15 miles. We show the positive spillover effect on housing price growth is more pronounced for relocating firms with larger employee sizes and economic bases. We further find local economic spillover and agglomeration economies associated with corporate relocation. Overall, our study indicates that corporate relocation exhibits a significant impact on the local housing market.

author.Maggie (Rong) Hu: Zicklin School of Business, Baruch College, City University of New York, New York, New York 10010
author.Desmond Tsang: School of Hotel and Tourism Management, Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, Hong Kong
author.Wayne Xinwei Wan: Department of Banking and Finance, Monash University, Clayton, Victoria 3800, Australia

article.1. Introduction: Headquarters have been an integral part of a firm (Mintzberg 1992), and relocating a company’s headquarters is an important corporate decision. In general, firms relocate their headquarters when the perceived benefits outweigh the expected cost of moving. A large strand of literature examines the rationales for headquarters relocation, and the general consensus is that firms would relocate for lower operating expenses, more resources, lower taxes, or a more highly agglomerated economy (Evans 1973,Burns 1977,Erickson and Wasylenko 1980,Garcia-Mila and McGuire 2002,Lovely et al. 2005,Klier 2006,Davis and Henderson 2008,Strauss-Kahn and Vives 2009,Voget 2011). Prior studies also document a positive capital market reaction when the relocation is value enhancing (Alli et al. 1991,Chan et al. 1995,Ghosh et al. 1995,Tirtiroglu et al. 2004). The relocation of corporate headquarters is different from that of other offices and plants, as headquarters is the coordination hub with a concentration of top-level executives and high-skill employees (Aksoy and Marshall 1992). Hence, corporate headquarters relocation entails an influx of skilled labor to the new location. Relocated corporations also engender local economic activities.Moretti (2010)finds a multiplier effect of additional jobs created in local goods and services markets when a new business enters the region. Moreover, headquarters lend prestige to their host locations and help attract related businesses to the areas (Tesla 2006,Kerr and Robert-Nicoud 2020,Hayes 2022). Consequently, the effects of corporate headquarters could result from labor migration, increased labor demand from relocating firms, and local economic spillover (Moretti 2010,Butler et al. 2019), as well as agglomeration economies (Aksoy and Marshall 1992,Davis and Henderson 2008,Saiz and Wachter 2011,Coulson et al. 2013). Yet, empirical evidence on the economic effects of corporate relocations is scant in prior literature. To fill this gap, this paper examines how corporate headquarters relocation impacts the local economy, with a particular focus on the residential property market of the relocation destinations. The residential property market offers a unique setting for two reasons. First, housing is not only a consumption good, but is also used for speculation and investment purposes (Gao et al. 2019), which enables market participants to act early to profit from the information of the relocation announcements (Barbanel 2018), thus leading to a distributional economic effect on households. Second, real estate properties are spatially distributed and location specific, generating variations in the impact of relocation events across space. This distinctive feature allows us to evaluate the influence of corporate relocation in a geographical context. Anecdotal evidence confirms that corporate headquarters relocation exerts a significant impact on the local housing market in the destination region, which could manifest even before the actual relocation event and extend to nearby areas. For instance, when Amazon announced in 2018 that it would build its second headquarters (HQ2), many cities submitted bids in the hope that HQ2 would boost local economic activities. Its economic impacts were clearly evident in Seattle, where Amazon’s first headquarters is located, with Amazon causing skyrocketing housing prices that, in turn, lowered the supply of affordable housing (Garfield 2018). Although Amazon has rebutted these criticisms, contending that there is no evidence to imply any direct correlation between Amazon and local home prices (Passy 2019), the local housing market was booming as soon as Arlington, Virginia, was announced as HQ2’s location. Housing prices started to rise in Arlington County and the surrounding areas immediately after the announcement at the end of 2018 and long before Amazon’s arrival. By the end of 2019, one year after Amazon’s announcement, residential house prices in Arlington County had already increased by 33%, according to the financial news website Market Watch. However, Amazon is not just any firm, given its size and the scale of its influence. Moreover, the location decision related to Amazon’s HQ2 involved an unconventional bidding process (Chen et al. 2024). Thus, housing price growth observed in its new headquarters location and the surrounding areas may not be generalizable to other firms’ headquarters relocations. Nonetheless, the importance of headquarters locations, and hence relocations, may have diminished due to globalization and the decentralization of corporations (Aksoy and Marshall 1992). Furthermore, some corporations may have relocated only on paper for tax reasons (Voget 2011). Last, the net effect of corporate headquarters relocation on the local economy and housing market is not obvious if existing firms are crowded out from the local area.1 In this study, we strive to provide a better understanding on this issue by documenting rich empirical evidence based on a large sample of U.S. firms’ headquarters relocations. As corporate headquarters relocation is expected to bring along an influx of labor and increased labor demand, we first estimate the general economic effect of corporate relocations on housing prices. Importantly, in addition to examining the contemporaneous effect of corporate relocations, we also assess the temporal effect of how the impact of corporate relocation extends to the period before, due to investor expectation of the event, and the period after, due to an increase in real housing demand. Furthermore, we investigate how the effect of relocation spreads from the headquarters locations to nearby areas due to spatial spillover. Last, we examine the mechanisms through which corporate relocations influence the residential property market. We examine the labor and capital influx of the relocated headquarters, as well as the impacts of local economic spillover and agglomeration economies induced by the relocation events. In so doing, our study considers important aspects of corporate relocation that extend beyond the mere increases in labor demand brought upon by the relocated firms. To offer a comprehensive analysis of the relationship between corporate headquarters relocation and the local housing market, we formulate a strategy to obtain a large sample of relocation events in the United States based on firms’ business addresses in U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings. Specifically, we identify corporate relocation events in the United States over the period from 1994 to 2017, with headquarters location data collected from Augmented 10-X Header Data in SEC filings.2Our evidence reveals that corporate headquarters relocation, while not a common occurrence, is hardly a rare event. Over the sample period, the number of corporate relocations is 16,191, and the average annual rate of corporate relocation is 7.92%. We measure annual changes in local housing prices by districts at the zip code level from 1997 to 2017, using data from the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) of All Homes. Our baseline results reveal that corporate relocations are associated with significantly higher housing price growth in districts where the new headquarters are located. The effect remains after controlling for time and geographic fixed effects and the macroeconomic and sociodemographic fundamentals that could affect housing prices and corporate relocation decisions. In the year of relocation, housing price growth rates increase by 0.35% for zip codes with a corporate headquarters relocation. The impact is economically significant, translating into about 10% higher housing price growth, based on an average annual house price growth rate of 3.506%, for zip codes with at least one corporate headquarters relocation compared with those without any in that year. We also find that the positive effect on local housing price increases with the number of headquarters that relocate into a zip code. The standard Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression estimating the impact of relocation event on housing price growth faces several endogeneity challenges. First, the relocation decision could be the outcome, instead of the determinant, of housing price growth in the region (Adelino et al. 2015). Second, certain latent factors could correlate with the corporate relocation decision and affect local housing price dynamics at the same time, while the direction of bias could be ambiguous. For example,Ghosh et al. (1995)document that corporates relocate due to various reasons: Apart from business-related incentives like cost savings, benefits of agglomeration economies, or proximity to the market, corporate relocation may also be promoted by managers’ self-interests for living and working in better districts. In addition, subsidies of relocation offered by the government may be correlated with local public expenditure that boosts housing price growth (Chow et al. 2022). On the other hand, as firms choose locations with the greatest expected net present value of future cashflows, they may prefer new headquarter locations that minimize their moving costs or future rents. In these situations, firms may prefer locations with lower housing price growth. Although time and geographic fixed effects in our OLS model capture respectively the time-variant factors at the national level and unobserved time-invariant factors of locations, they cannot control for the time-variant latent variables in each location. To address these endogeneity concerns, we have come up with two identification strategies. First, we match on zip code characteristics to address unobserved district heterogeneity. For those zip codes with relocation events, we create a matched sample from those without relocation events using the propensity score matching (PSM) method (Becker and Ichino 2002,Abadie and Imbens 2006,Campello et al. 2010). Specifically, we match zip codes on macroeconomic, sociodemographic, geographic factors, and time trends, as well as state-level corporate income tax changes that have been shown to induce relocation decisions (Heider and Ljungqvist 2015,Chow et al. 2022). We further adopt different matching techniques to ensure the robustness of our findings. Our results remain qualitatively the same using the PSM method. Second, we use a Bartik instrumental variable (IV) approach to address endogeneity. We calculate a shift-share prediction of relocated firms based on the number of relocated firms in each industryover the United Statesand thehistoricalindustry distribution of existing headquartersin each zip codeand use this predicted value as the instrument for the actual relocation (Card 2001,2009;Ottaviano and Peri 2006;Saiz 2007;Saiz and Wachter 2011;Jaeger et al. 2019). The predicted number of relocations, calculated from historical industrial distributions and aggregated number of relocations at the national level, is unlikely to directly affect future housing price changes at the zip code level apart from its correlation with the actual relocation (Goldsmith-Pinkham et al. 2020). Under the IV approach, we continue to obtain a consistently positive impact of relocations on housing price growth. Next, we examine temporal effects of relocations on housing prices. Relocation events are commonly announced in advance (Gregory and Lombard 2005), so local housing prices may start increasing prior to the actual relocation events. Housing prices may also continue to increase after the relocation, due to surges in local economic activities and agglomeration economies that result from the firm’s relocation. We thus examine the pre- and postrelocation impact of corporate headquarters relocation from the year before to the year after the relocation. We find that housing prices increase by 0.29% in the year before the relocation (t− 1), which demonstrates an expectation effect for labor employment and local economic activities brought upon by theactualrelocation events. In the year after relocation (t+ 1), housing prices continue to increase by 0.34%, which signifies the prolonged economic impact of corporate relocation on the local housing market. When we extend the pre- and postrelocation impact to longer time windows around the relocation event, we further find that housing prices increase in yeart+ 2 by another 0.23% after relocation. The cumulative effect from yeart− 1 tot+ 2 translates into more than 30% higher growth in housing prices for zip codes with corporate relocations than those without relocations. Overall, these findings indicate that a corporate relocation event exerts a strong effect on the housing market due to theexpectation effectbefore the relocation, as well as thereal effectfrom the surges in labor demand and local economic activities induced by the arrival of the relocated headquarters. The economic benefits that arise from the relocated headquarters could extend from the receiving zip code to the surrounding areas, which prompts us to examine the spatial spillover effect of corporate relocation on nearby zip codes. We find that the positive spillover effect of corporate relocation indeed extends to neighboring regions, with the impact being the strongest for up to 5 miles from the relocated headquarters. The impact is economically significant and reflects about 19.6% higher growth in those zip codes compared with those without any relocation event. Furthermore, we show that the impact of the relocated headquarters is significant on local housing markets up to 15 miles from the new headquarters location, although its impact is decreasing in distance, consistent with the spatial attenuation of agglomeration economies (Rosenthal and Strange 2020). We next investigate the potential channels through which corporate headquarters relocation affects the local housing market. We first examine how corporate headquarters relocation influences housing prices with the influx of labor, as well as capital investment that engenders labor demand (Coulson et al. 2013).Collis et al. (2007,2012) show headquarters size is positively correlated with a firm’s total number of employees and economic base. Along this line, we examine the effects of relocating firms’ employee sizes and economic bases on housing price growth. We find confirming evidence that the impact of headquarters relocation in a zip code is more pronounced when the relocating firms have higher total numbers of employees and larger combined sizes. A second channel that corporate relocations boost housing price growth could be through a multiplier effect on the local goods and services markets (Moretti 2010). We follow a similar methodology as inButler et al. (2019)and examine the impact of corporate relocations on the local area’s total employment, total establishments, and retail establishments. Our findings show headquarters relocation is associated with increased total employment, total establishments, and retail establishments in a zip code. Last, we explore a third channel of the impact of corporate headquarters relocation through agglomeration economies. The benefits of agglomeration derive from the synergies of related businesses located in proximity, given the cost reductions and gains in efficiency that result from such proximity (Abdel-Rahman 1990,Duranton and Puga 2001,Dougal et al. 2015). We document an agglomeration effect, as higher housing price growth is observed when a firm relocates to a zip code with more corporate headquarters from thesameindustry, and when the relocating firm belongs to thesameindustry as one of the top five major industries in the region. We supplement our main analysis with a subsample analysis using a hand-collected sample of relocation announcements for S&P 500 firms. Our analysis shows that housing prices increase for zip codes with incoming headquarters relocations onlyafterthe relocation announcements, butnot before, indicating there is little information leakage and speculation before public information release, and confirming that the expectation effect is the dominant reason for housing price increase before the actual relocation events. Moreover, we show the housing price effect sustains after the actual relocation events, again confirming with the prolonged real effect of corporate relocation. In addition, we infer the rationales of corporate relocations from stock market responses of relocation announcements (Ghosh et al. 1995) and classify the relocations into two types: (1) cost saving and (2) managerial preference. We find that both types of relocations exert positive impact on housing price growth, consistent with our baseline result. Furthermore, we show that those relocations related to managerial preference–based reason is associated with larger housing price growth on average than those of the cost-saving reason, possibly because manager-induced relocations are sorted into districts with better latent amenities, albeit the difference is not statistically significant. In an additional analysis, we investigate the simultaneous impact of corporate headquarters moving in and out of a zip code on the local housing market. Although we continue to find a significant positive association between corporate headquarters moving into a zip code and changes in housing prices, we observe a marginally negative but much smaller impact of headquarters moving out of a zip code before the relocation event. This finding implies that, for the zip codes welcoming a new headquarter, the positive spillover effect of a corporate relocation event outweighs its negative crowding out effect. Finally, we conduct several heterogenous analyses with respect to relocation distances and different tiers of the local housing market. We find that cross-state relocations (that entail more human capital relocations and higher demand for local input) have larger impacts than relocations across cities in the same state or across zip codes within a city. Moreover, we show that housing price increases more in proportion for top-tier homes compared with bottom-tier homes, demonstrating a distributional effect within the residential property market due to the influx of skilled labor at corporate headquarters. Overall, our study contributes to the literature by showing that headquarters relocation is an important corporate event with a significant spillover effect on the local housing market. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to provide comprehensive evidence on the housing price effects of corporate headquarters relocation events using the full relocation records. We add to the scant literature that examines how corporate decisions and performance affect local housing market dynamics where the firm headquarters is located. A contemporaneous study byChen et al. (2024)examines the Amazon HQ2 location announcement and its unique bidding process. We differ from their study by providing large-sample general evidence of how a relocation event exerts temporal and spatial spillover effects to the housing market.Coulson et al. (2013)show that earnings projections for corporations in an Metropolitan statistical area (MSA) positively predict future house prices in that MSA. They interpret their results as follows: Projected earnings growth for corporations in a MSA forecasts the demand for labor—which, in turn, anticipates the demand in that MSA for housing of the new labor.Joslin and Konchitchki (2018)also show changes in the profitability of firms headquartered within a region provide implications for regional real estate valuation. We extend these studies by explicitly demonstrating the spillover effects due to increased local economic activities and economies of agglomeration, through which corporate relocations could create multiplier effects that further enhance housing demand. In broader terms, our study also adds to the growing literature that examines the macroeconomic consequences and economic value of the corporate entry and exit in a region. For instance,Pope and Pope (2015)show that opening a Walmart store increases nearby housing prices after opening. However, the announcement effect before the official opening is only marginally significant, which indicates that there is little expectation effect associated with Walmart openings. Moreover, the spatial spillover effect of a Walmart opening is relatively small and limited to within 1 mile of the store’s location. Our study documents a much more significant effect of a corporate relocation event than a Walmart opening.Adelino et al. (2017)show that the opening of startups, rather than the expansion of existing firms, is responsible for most of the employment growth in a region.Greenstone et al. (2010)find that the productivity of a region, measured by the total factor productivity of incumbent plants in an area, is higher with the entry of a large manufacturing plant in the same area.Bernstein et al. (2019)show that the bankruptcy of an establishment in an area has an adverse effect on the local economy, which leads to lower growth of existing establishments and reduced entry of new establishments. We contribute to this line of literature by documenting a significant corporate relocation effect on the local economy. In addition, we explore the interesting aspects of how headquarters relocation results in spillover effects on the local economy, and in so doing, we demonstrate thegeneraleconomic value of having a cluster of firm headquarters. Last, this study offers important implications for policymakers and market practitioners. Our findings imply that local regions must carefully consider the unintended consequences when trying to attract corporate headquarters. Because corporate relocation events typically result in housing price appreciation, city and state policymakers must carefully weigh their potential benefits against the externality effects associated with local housing affordability and economic welfare. Although the housing boom resulting from corporate relocations could benefit existing homeowners, it may exacerbate the housing affordability issue for those who plan to buy homes. For real estate industry practitioners, our study implies that the local housing market could have large fluctuations due to the intertwined relationship between corporate entry and residential real estate prices. Hence, housing market forecasts should take corporate spatial decisions into consideration for real estate valuation. The rest of the paper is organized as follows.Section 2describes the sample selection process and presents descriptive statistics.Section 3presents the empirical design and main results of our analyses.Section 4presents additional and robustness analyses, andSection 5concludes.
article.2. Data and Sample: 2.1. Sample Construction and Key Measures We obtain our sample of corporate headquarters relocation events from 1994 to 2017. The information on headquarters location is collected from the Augmented 10-X Header Data, which captures all the information in the header section of 10-K/Q SEC filings on EDGAR. There are 1,285,447 filings for 42,368 firms with a unique Central Index Key (CIK) over the sample period. In each filing, we obtain the headquarters location from the firm’s business address, from which we then extract the corresponding information on the state, city, and district by the five-digit zip code. Firms with incorrect business addresses and firms with incorrect state abbreviations are eliminated from the sample. Our final sample contains 1,194,158 company filings with valid headquarters information. To identify the incidence of a corporate headquarters relocation, we track those changes in the business address in firms’ SEC filings over the years, following the methodology ofCalluzzo et al. (2021)andChow et al. (2022). Instead of relying only on 10-K filings, as in prior studies, we improve on their methodology by also examining 10-Q filings, which provide richer information for our identification. Although the exact month of the relocation is not observable, using the change in business address provides a reasonably good identification strategy, especially because our data also contain the quarterly reports (i.e., the 10-Q filings) when available. We use the business address in each firm’sfirstfiling (10-K/Q) in yeartas the initial headquarters location for the firm in yeart. If the firm is found to have two different business addresses across two consecutive filings during yeart, we consider that the firm has relocated its headquarters in that year. If the firm has a different business address in the first filing in yeart+ 1, we also consider that the firm has relocated its headquarters in yeart.3Our final sample contains 204,373 firm-year observations with headquarters information from 29,183 firms from 1994 to 2017. We obtain firms’ fundamental financial data from the Compustat database. We measure changes in housing prices at the zip code level, given that housing market spillovers are usually highly localized (Arzaghi and Henderson 2008,Rosenthal and Strange 2020). Following prior studies that examine local housing markets (Raymond et al. 2016,Lang 2018,Anenberg and Kung 2020,Brown and Matsa 2020), we use the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) of All Homes to measure housing prices at the zip code level. The monthly zip code level housing price index has been available since April 1996, and we use the index for December of each year as the calendar year-end housing price. The annual growth of the housing price in yeartis thus calculated as the change in the housing price in logarithmic form from yeart− 1 to yeart. We obtain a sample of 347,446 zip code-year observations from 1996 to 2017. Since our empirical analysis requires data on housing price changes, our final regression sample covers the period between 1997 and 2017, with 315,137 zip code-year observations. We control for several important macroeconomic and sociodemographic variables in our multivariate analyses. Personal income, at the core-based statistical area (CBSA) level, is obtained from the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA). Population and unemployment rate, available at the state level, are obtained from FRED Economic Data. We further obtain geographic information at the zip code level from ESRI Limited, and local employment and establishment data at the zip code level from U.S. Census Bureau’s ZIP Code Business Patterns (ZBP). 2.2. The overall percentage of corporate headquarters relocation is 7.92%, and the total number of corporate relocations is 16,191. We further divide the relocation events into corporations that relocated across states, across cities, and across districts at the zip code level. Of the 7.92% of firms that relocated their headquarters, an average of 3.10% per year relocated across states, 2.85% across cities within the same state, and 1.97% across zip codes within the same city. article.3. Method and Results: 3.1. Contemporaneous Effect of Headquarters Relocation on Housing Price 3.1.1. Baseline OLS Estimation. In this study, we propose that corporate headquarters relocation exerts a significant impact on the local housing market. We first examine and quantify the economic effect of corporate relocation on contemporaneous local housing price changes in the year of relocation by estimating the following equation: The dependent variable,ΔLog (Pricei,t), measures the local housing price changes for zip codeiin yeart. The key variable of interest inEquation (1)isRelocationi,t, an indicator variable that equals one if at least one corporate headquarters relocates into the zip code in the year (zero otherwise). We expect the coefficient ofb1to be positive and statistically significant if corporate relocation exerts a positive effect on the local housing market. Alternatively, we runEquation (1)withRelocation Numberi,t, which represents the number of corporate relocation events in zip codeiin yeart. We control for several macroeconomic and sociodemographic fundamentals that affect the housing market: income, population, and employment (Butler et al. 2019;Cornaggia et al. 2019). It is also important to control for these factors because corporate relocation decisions might correlate with local economic and sociodemographic factors. In our analyses, we control for the logarithmic forms ofPersonal Incomei,tandPopulationi,t, as well as theUnemployment Ratei,t.We expect that personal income and population have positive impacts on the housing market, while unemployment has a negative impact. To control for time trend and other unobserved time-invariant location characteristics, we also include time (year) and geographic (CBSA) fixed effects in all our regressions, denoted respectively as𝜑𝑡φtand𝜇𝑖μi.𝑒𝑖,𝑡ei,trepresents the error term. For our baseline empirical analysis, we estimateEquation (1)using OLS regressions. In all regressions, we control for potential cross-correlations by clustering robust standard errors at the city level. Panel A ofTable 4reports the estimation results. In columns (1) and (2), we report the regression estimation results ofEquation (1)without and with the control variables, respectively. The results show that the coefficients onRelocationi,tare positive (statistically significant at the 1% level) in both columns, which imply that the housing price growth rates for zip codes that have at least one corporate headquarters relocation event are higher than for those without relocations. The coefficients forb1in columns (1) and (2) are 0.356 and 0.352, which mean that housing prices increase by 0.356% and 0.352% in response to the relocation events, respectively. Because the average annual housing price change is 3.506%, these translate into 10.2% and 10.0% incremental increases in annual housing price growth for zip codes with corporate headquarters relocation compared with those without. Propensity Score Matching Estimation. Corporate headquarters relocation decisions may be driven by some unobserved desirability of a location that also affects housing prices. Hence, our baseline results could be explained as corporations being attracted to zip codes with strong fundamentals that also fuse housing price growth. Relatedly, reverse causality may also be present as relocation decisions could be the outcome of rising or falling housing prices in the region.Adelino et al. (2015)show that MSAs with the highest housing price increases experience the largest increases in establishment growth. Cities with a booming local economy could in turn attract more firms to place their headquarters. Alternatively, headquarters could be attracted to districts with lower housing price for cost-saving reason. To alleviate these endogeneity concerns, we implement two identification strategies. First, we use the propensity score matching approach to match each relocation zip code with a nonrelocation zip code with similar characteristics. We estimate a propensity score (i.e., the likelihood of relocation for each zip code) based on a set of observable characteristics, including macroeconomic and sociodemographic factors (i.e., personal income, population, and unemployment rate) and CBSA and year fixed effects. In addition, as documented byChow et al. (2022)andHeider and Ljungqvist (2015), corporate income tax movements also impact corporate relocation decisions, so we include control for the state level corporate income tax change,ΔCorporate Income Taxi,t, measured as the change of state corporate income tax rate from the previous year to the current year. In the first stage, we regressRelocationi,ton these fundamental variables using Probit regressions and generate a propensity score for each zip code-year observation. The result is reported in column (1) ofTable 4, Panel B. We then match each zip code in a given year that has at least one corporate relocation event with those without such event based on the closest estimated propensity scores. We conduct the nearest neighbor one-to-one matching with replacement but include all best matches with identical scores in the event of a tie. We also impose a common support criterion by dropping treatment observations with propensity scores higher than the maximum or lower than the minimum scores of the control group. To validate the matching quality, we followCampello et al. (2010)and conduct further balance tests to check whether zip codes with a relocation event (the treatment sample) and the matched ones without a relocation event (the control sample) have balanced distributions in propensity scores.Figure 2presents the propensity scores of the treatment and control samples before and after matching. Finally, we estimate the treatment effect inEquation (1)using the matched subsamples (Abadie and Imbens 2006). In columns (2) and (3) of Panel B, we present the second-stage estimation results for the matched sample tests. We find that our main findings remain intact under the PSM matched sample design. As the PSM analysis hinges on some methodological choices, we perform additional sensitivity analysis on the PSM to ensure the robustness of our findings. The results are presented in the Online Appendix, Table A.3. Panel A presents the second-stage estimation results, after the same calculation of propensity scores in the first stage, but by using alternative matching methods without replacement, or year-by-year matching, to obtain the matched subsamples. In Panel B, we replicate the analysis but obtain the propensity scores using a linear probability model to account for the potential bias in fixed-effect estimators of nonlinear panel data models (Neyman and Scott 1948). In Panel C, we include the county level property tax rate, obtained from the American Community Survey (ACS), as an additional determinant of relocation decisions.6We observe positive and statistically significant relocation effects on housing price growth for all these matched sample tests, which indicate the robustness of our PSM results. 3.1.3. Instrumental Variable Estimation. In our second identification strategy, we use a Bartik IV approach to address the endogeneity concern. Ideally, the IV should have a strong correlation with the intensity of headquarters relocations in that zip code in yeartbut does not directly affect local economic activity and housing prices beyond this correlation. We follow the methodology inBartik (1991)7and predict the number of headquarters relocating into a zip code in yeartusing the shift-share approach. Specifically, for each industry,8we use the number of headquarters in a zip code in the earliest base year of 1996, relative to the nation’s total number of firms in that industry, as the share in the zip code. We then calculateshift-sharein the zip code, following similar methodology inCard (2009), as the product of this share measure in the base year and the total number of relocating firms in the same industryacross the United Statesin yeart.9The predicted number of relocated headquarters in the zip code is equal to thesum of the shift-sharesacross all industries. We use this shift-share predictor as an instrument forRelocationi,tin the first-stage regressions. We further illustrate the calculation of our Bartik IV measure using the zip code 94303 in Palo Alto, California, as an example. In 1996, there were five firms in the technology industry with their headquarters located at this location, and there were in total 1,331 firms in the technology industry nationwide. Therefore, this zip code has an industry concentration of 0.38% in the technology industry. In 2015, there are 67 relocations of technology firms from other zip codes nationwide. Therefore, the predicted number of technology firms moving into this zip code in 2015 equals 0.25 (0.38% × 67). Similarly, there are six firms in the life science industry with their headquarters located at this location in 1996, and there are in total 1,023 firms in the life science industry nationwide. Therefore, this zip code has an industry concentration of 0.59% in the life science industry. In 2015, there are 90 relocations of life science firms from other zip codes nationwide, so the predicted number of technology firms moving into this zip code in 2015 equals 0.53 (0.39% × 90). We repeat this process for all industries to calculate the total predicted relocations into this zip code. The predicted number of relocated headquarters based on the shift-share approach is an appropriate instrument for our identification as it satisfies both assumptions of relevance and exclusion restrictions. First, it is highly relevant to the actual relocation events because existing industry distribution would attract firms in the same industry to relocate into the zip code due to the agglomeration effect. Panel A of Table A.4 in the Online Appendix summarizes the zip codes’ average industry shares. It shows that zip codes with a higher concentration of headquarters in an industry are more likely to attract relocating firms in that industry. Overall, the average predicted number of relocations using the base year of 1996 forallzip codes is 0.044. For zip codes with actual relocations, the average predicted number of relocations is 0.687. For zip codes without actual relocations, the average number is 0.024. Similar findings are observed when we calculate industry shares using the previous yeart− 1 as an alternate base year. The substantially higher predicted numbers in zip codes with actual relocations compared with those without lend support to our procedures in approximating actual relocations. Second, the exclusion restriction is satisfied because the predicted number of relocations is likely exogenous to anytime-variantfactors affecting housing prices beyond its association with the actual relocation.10The predicted number of relocations is calculated based onhistoricalindustrial shares and total number of relocations in yeartat the national level. Prior studies have provided theoretical support that the historical industry shares are likely exogenous to the changes in the outcome variable, conditional on using geographic fixed effects to capture thetime-invariantimpact of location characteristics and time fixed effects to capture the national-leveltime-variantfactors (Saiz 2007,Sá 2015). This is because the residuals in housing price growth in such a model are mainly resulted from thetime-variantfactors at the local level, whereas the historical industry shares in the early base year are unlikely to be correlated with the localtime-variantfactors in future years, apart from their associations with actual firm relocations.11In other words, we assume “random” historical shares to future changes in the outcome variable (Goldsmith-Pinkham et al. 2020). Moreover, the aggregated number of relocations at the national level is likely to be exogenous to local economic conditions at the zip code level.12Hence, our predicted number of relocations, calculated based on these two components, should be exogenous to future changes in housing price growth, apart from its correlation with the actual relocation. The first-stage IV results are presented in columns (1) and (2) ofTable 4, Panel C. They show that the Bartik predictor is highly positively correlated with the test variableRelocationi,tin both regression specifications (significant at the 1% level), again supporting the relevance of the instrument. TheFstatistics for the first-stage estimations are close to 200, which demonstrate that our instrument is strong. We then report the second-stage IV estimation results in columns (3) and (4) of Panel C. We continue to observe a significant impact of corporate relocation on housing price changes. In zip codes with relocation events, the housing price increases by 1.56%. Because the average growth rate in housing price is 3.506%, this translates to a 44% increase in the growth rate. The IV estimates are larger than the OLS estimates inTable 4, Panel A. It implies that on average, the main endogeneity in our OLS estimates originates from the fact that firms tend to select new headquarters locations with lower housing prices to minimize their moving cost, holding everything else equal. In other words, our OLS estimates serve as a lower bound of the real impact of headquarters relocation on housing prices. We conduct a set of additional analyses to ensure our IV estimation results are reliable. First, to ensure our estimates are not unduly influenced by misspecification in a certain subset of industries, we present the Rotemberg weights of industries in the Online Appendix, Table A.4, Panel C (Rotemberg 1983). Specifically, the weight of an industry represents how much potential misspecification for that industry could be translated into the overall bias of the estimator (Conley et al. 2012). We find that the distribution of our Rotemberg weights is relatively uniform across industries, with the largest weight at 0.25 for the trade and services industry. This implies that our Bartik instrument estimator is unlikely to be severely influenced due to potential misspecifications in some industries. Second, becauseGoldsmith-Pinkham et al. (2020)show that a Bartik IV is numerically equivalent to using the shift-shares as multiple instruments, we conduct an overidentification test by using individual industry shares as IVs. The regression results are presented in the Online Appendix, Table A.4, Panel D. We obtain similar findings as our main Bartik IV results. Also, the overidentification tests fail to reject as the Sargan’s J statistics are insignificant. These findings further alleviate the concern for misspecification in our Bartik instrument. Last, we use therollinghistorical shares from the previous yeart− 1 as an alternative approach to construct the Bartik IV, which helps to alleviate the selection issue that some industries/districts may have missing historical shares in the early base year. Following this approach, we recalculate the predicted relocations number using the historical shares in the previous year as the base shares. We obtain similar findings using this alternative instrument, as reported in the Online Appendix, Table A.4, Panel E. It indicates that our IV estimation results are robust. 3.2. Temporal Effect of Headquarters Relocation on Housing Price The impact of corporate headquarters relocation could extend to the period before the relocation because of an expectation effect. Moreover, the increase in local economic activities and agglomeration economies could have real prolonged effect on the housing market in the subsequent period. InFigure 3, we plot the average annual growth rate in housing price for zip codes with a corporate headquarters relocating into the zip code (a) compared with those without any corporate headquarters relocation (b), controlling for local economic trends and over the period from eight years before to five years after the relocation event.13Panel A clearly demonstrates the presence of an expectation effect due to the relocation event because the average local housing price growth rate jumps noticeably in about two years before the relocation event.14This finding is consistent with the anecdotal evidence regarding Amazon’s HQ2 and demonstrates housing price increases after the relocation decision is announced to the public but well before the actual relocation event. On the other hand, we observe no significant change in housing price growth for zip codes without any headquarters relocation. In the years after a relocation event, we observe that the average housing price growth remains higher in zip codes with corporate relocation events, and it gradually reverts to a level closer to that of zip codes without a corporate relocation as time goes by. This finding documents the prolonged effect of headquarters relocation, which could be due to corporations moving their headquarters in phases or to the delayed effects of increased local economic activities and agglomeration. Nonetheless, it implies that corporate headquarters relocation induces a real economic boost to the local housing market. Spatial Effect of Headquarters Relocation on Housing Price The impact of corporate headquarters relocation could also extend to nearby locations due to a spatial spillover effect, which implies the far-reaching impact corporate relocation events have on the local housing market. The boost to the local housing market could also be due to agglomeration economies, as firms benefit from talent pool and knowledge spillover by locating in proximity to each other. Some firms are seen as drivers of local economic activity not only because they provide exogenous spending but also because they act as triggers for agglomeration (Tesla 2006,Davis and Henderson 2008). The prestige and branding of headquarters in one region may help attract more investment and a better workforce (Tesla 2006).Shilton and Stanley (1999), for example, show that locations with more corporate headquarters are associated with higher income per capita. When more firms in related business fields cluster together, their costs of production may also decline significantly due to competing multiple suppliers, greater specialization, and division of labor. As agglomeration derives predominantly from synergies with related businesses (Abdel-Rahman 1990,Duranton and Puga 2001,Dougal et al. 2015), we next examine the agglomeration effect when the relocated and existing firms in the zip code belong to the same industry. We test our conjecture by estimating the following equation: ForEquation (8), the variable of interest is the interaction term ofRelocationi,tandExisting HQ Same Industryi,t−1, which equals one if the relocated firm belongs to the same industry as at least one of the firms with existing headquarters in the same zip code (zero otherwise). A location could only possibly have an existing headquartersin the same industryas the relocated firm when there was a relocation event (i.e.,Relocationi,t= 1)andthe zip code had existing headquarters (i.e.,Existing HQi,t−1= 1). We conjecture that agglomeration could further increase prices in the local housing market when the relocated firm belongs to the same industry as the existing firms in the zip code. We also find thatExisting HQi,t−1has a positive impact on increasing local housing prices. This finding corresponds toShilton and Stanley (1999), who show that the number of headquarters is positively associated with the per capita income of a region. Overall, these results are consistent with prior studies (Dougal et al. 2015), whereby agglomeration benefits from firms in similar industries that locate in proximity to each other. However, our findings show that this agglomeration effect extends beyond industries and is also apparent for the local economy, and it exerts a positive spillover effect on the housing market. This channel also coincides with the observed significant but decreasing spatial spillover effect in nearby zip codes, because agglomeration economies attenuate in space (Rosenthal and Strange 2020).
article.4. Additional Analyses and Robustness Ch: 4.1. Relocation Announcements In this section, we further disentangle the prerelocation effect based on the dates of the relocation announcements. We hand-collect news related to the headquarters relocation from Factiva for the S&P 500 firms over our sample period. We obtain 198 relocation events19and record their announcement dates as the earliest HQ relocation announcements obtained from Factiva. InFigure 4, we plot the average annual housing price growth rate for zip codes with relocations, centered at the announcement dateinsteadof the actual relocation event. Using the same method as inFigure 3, we report average housing price growth after controlling for macroeconomic and sociodemographic factors and time and geographic fixed effects and over the period from eight years before the relocation announcement to five years after. The figure shows housing price growthonlystarts to increase after the announcement date, until about four years after the announcement year, but there is little speculation effect before the announcements. In addition, the magnitudes of the effects of these S&P firms inFigure 4are larger than the average effects of all firms we observe inFigure 3, probably because firms with relocation announcements recorded by Factiva tend to be larger firms and industry leaders with higher industry shares.20As supporting evidence, Panel A ofTable 10provides univariate comparisons of the sizes and industry shares of the S&P 500 firms with relocation events for which we could and could not obtain relocation announcements from Factiva. This finding aligns with our heterogeneity analysis results that headquarters relocations of firms with larger economic bases exert larger spillovers to the housing market. Moving Out Effect of Headquarters Relocation If a corporate relocation event leads to housing price appreciation in the zip code with an incoming headquarters relocation, but its crowding-out effect leads to housing price depreciation due to an outgoing headquarter, such an event could be a zero-sum game. We conduct an additional analysis to examine the simultaneous impact of corporate headquarters moving in and out of a zip code on the local housing market. Heterogeneity Analyses We conduct further heterogenous analyses to examine conditions in which corporate headquarters relocation events could exert differential impacts on the local housing market. First, we examine the heterogeneous impact of relocation on the local housing market across relocation distances.Gregory and Lombard (2005)argue that relocation distance matters, because short-distance relocation is local in nature and has little impact on the firm and its employees, whereas long-distance relocation could induce corporate upheaval because it creates the most disruptions, such as changing vendors for local services and relocating employees. We classify all relocations into relocations across states, across cities, and across zip codes.24We expect the impact to be strongest for relocations across states, because such relocations would most likely involve relocating existing employees, hiring new employees, increases in demand for local inputs, and agglomeration economies with firms at the new location. To test this impact, we modify our baselineEquation (1)by replacingRelocationi,twith a series of explanatory variables to indicate relocation events across states, cities, or zip codes, respectively. In column (1) ofTable 12, we present the estimation results. We find that the impact on local housing market returns is statistically significant predominantly for relocations across states (at the 1% level), but less so for relocations across cities within the same state or across zip codes within the same city. The results confirm our expectation that the impact on the local housing market is the strongest when the corporate relocation event brings a remote headquarters into the zip code. In contrast, headquarters that are already located in the same state or city do not seem to exert the same impact on the local housing market. These findings indicate that the positive spillover effect of corporate headquarters relocation could be limited if a firm is only moving the corporate headquarters to a nearby zip code or city, as it is without a need for its employees to relocate or to change vendors for local input demand. Additional Robustness Checks In robustness analysis, we further alleviate unobserved heterogeneity and endogeneity concerns by including alternative fixed effects in the regression models. In our main analysis, we include controls for time and CBSA fixed effects. However, some influential observed or unobserved factors, such as state taxation, may vary at the state level. Although the CBSA fixed effects should encompass most unobserved variations across states, we replicateEquation (1)by replacing CBSA with state fixed effects. In addition, the time and geographic fixed effects in our baseline models could only control for time trend and time-invariant location features. To better capture the impact of unobserved time-variant factors that might influence relocation decisions, we further include the state-by-year fixed effects in the model as a robustness check. This method effectively addresses the potential confounding effect of government subsidies that are provided at the state level (Chow et al. 2022). Last, we capture zip code location-specific features by replacing CBSA with zip code fixed effects (with standard errors also clustered at the zip code level). These estimation results are presented in the Online Appendix, Table A.8, which indicate that our findings remain robust. We next control for the time-variant nature of the macroeconomic and demographic variables over time by including their de-trended variables (i.e., residuals after controlling for time trend and CBSA fixed effects) and applying the PSM approach. The results of the PSM including these de-trended control variables are presented in the Online Appendix, Table A.9. We obtain similar findings as our original PSM results, which support the robustness of our results. In addition, to deal with another potential endogeneity issue inEquation (1), whereby the previous housing price growth in a zip code could both impact firms’ relocation decisions and be serially correlated with housing price growth in later periods, we include prior housing price growth in yeart− 1 as an additional control variable. Also, studies on housing returns (Saiz 2010,Huang and Tang 2012,Butler et al. 2019) include variations in income, population, and unemployment as controls. We do not include population density in our main analysis, as it is highly correlated with population. Nor do we include urbanization rate, as this is available from the U.S. Census only every 10 years, and thus the same rate must be applied for the in-between years (Huang and Tang 2012). Nonetheless, we include these additional control variables as a robustness check. Our findings remain robust as presented in the Online Appendix, Table A.10. We also conduct an additional robustness analysis using the alternative identification of headquarters relocation events. Although the Augmented 10-X Header Data has headquarters location information that is updated each year, it does not report the exact dates of the relocation events. Noteworthy is that relocations could have occurred after the calendar year-end but before the first 10-K/Q filing in the next calendar year, which introduces a bias in our methodology whereby we measure contemporaneous house price changes before the actual relocation event date. To address this potential measurement issue, we adopt alternative identification strategies of relocation year forEquation (1). If the address remains unchanged in all filings in yeartbut is changed from the last filing in yeartto the first filing in yeart+ 1, we propose two alternative identification methods for robustness: (1) we designate the relocation as having occurred in the next year instead, and (2) we exclude these relocation events from our sample. These two methods generate results consistent with our main findings, and we report the estimation results in the Online Appendix, Table A.11. Finally, to ensure our findings are not driven by spurious correlation, we perform a permutation test by randomly assigning relocation events to zip code-year observations, and we estimate the pseudo treatment effect usingEquation (1). We repeat the process for 500 times, and we report the distribution of permutation estimates inFigure 5. It reflects that the permutation estimates from random assignments are centered around zero with a mean of −0.009 and a standard deviation of 0.074. The actual relocation effect fromTable 4, column (2), is 0.3520, which differs from the permutation distribution at the 1% level (p= 0.000). Therefore, it supports that our finding is not driven by spurious correlations.
article.5. Conclusion: This study implies that a corporate headquarters relocation decision could exert significant spillover effects on the local residential property market, across both time and space. We empirically quantify the general impact of a corporate headquarters relocation event on the housing market and find that corporate headquarters relocation is positively associated with local housing price changes, leading to 10% higher housing price growth in zip codes with a corporate relocation event. We adopt the propensity score matching and instrumental variable approaches to alleviate the unobserved endogeneity concerns, and our results remain intact. Importantly, we also document the temporal effect of corporate relocation, in which housing prices start to increase one year before the relocation event, indicating that at least some of the changes in housing prices are due to changes in expectations regarding future prices, which in turn leads to increased activities on the local housing market before any real economic boost related to the relocation events. Moreover, we observe a prolonged effect on the housing market beyond the year of relocation. Examining the spatial spillover effect of a corporate relocation event, we show that the impact of a corporate headquarters relocation can influence housing price growth for nearby zip codes up to 15 miles away. We examine the channels through which corporate relocation exerts an impact on the housing market. Notwithstanding our findings that indicate the influx of skilled labor and increased labor demand from the relocated firms boost housing price growth, we also observe enhanced effects from local economic spillover and economies of agglomeration. This study contributes to the literature by providing novel evidence of significant spillover effects from the corporate setting to the local housing market. We show that the channel of impact through the propagation of headquarters relocation decision on the demand for residential properties results in significant surges in local housing prices. Our focus on the housing market has enabled us to demonstrate both temporal and spatial impact of corporate decision making on the local housing market. Usually, when the capital market responds to corporate policy changes by one firm, the contagion effect at most extends to firms in a related industry or firms in proximity. Our findings show that such a contagion effect also spills over to the local housing market, and thereby affects the pricing of proximate residential properties. Our study bears significant implications for policymakers by highlighting the benefits as well as concerns regarding economic externalities in corporate relocation decisions. We show that corporate relocation has led to significant effects on the local housing markets, which could also pose greater challenges for housing affordability. Hence, corporate headquarters relocation, which engenders higher demand for local housing, could act as a double-edged sword that exerts a distributional effect and reduces housing affordability. Our findings also bear important implications for academics and practitioners. Housing price dynamics could have large fluctuations due to the expectation effect resulting from a corporate relocation event. While our study reinforces the notion that models of housing prices incorporate these forward-looking expectations regarding urban economic activity (Coulson et al. 2013), we provide novel evidence that these activities in the housing market could be driven by a corporate decision. Our study establishes an important linkage between corporate management and local housing price dynamics, and it implies that market participants should take corporate spatial decisions into consideration when forecasting housing prices.
